He complained under Article 6 § 1 of the Convention that the Independent Administrative Panel had failed to hold a hearing. Such a hearing would have been necessary because his appeal was not limited to points of law. He had also criticised the District Administrative Authority’s assessment of evidence. Therefore an oral hearing would have been necessary. In such a hearing the Independent Administrative Panel could have properly assessed this evidence, which had already been obtained at first instance, as well as fresh evidence.

On 13 July 1995 the Constitutional Court declined to deal with the applicant’s complaint on the ground that it did not have sufficient prospects of success. Upon a request filed by the applicant on 10 August 1995, the Constitutional Court transferred the case to the Administrative Court (Verwaltungsgerichtshof).

On 23 February 1996 the Administrative Court, relying on Section 33a of the Administrative Court Act (Verwaltungsgerichtshofgesetz), declined to deal with the applicant’s case, finding that it did not raise important legal issues. On 10 May 1996 this decision was served on the applicant’s lawyer.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 20089/06) against the Republic of Austria lodged with the Court under Article 34 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an Austrian national, Mr Hans Herbert Puchstein (“the applicant”), on 5 May 2006.

The applicant was represented by Mr M. Lechner, a lawyer practising in Lochau. The Austrian Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Ambassador F. Trauttmansdorff, Head of the International Law Department at the Federal Ministry for European and International Affairs.

The applicant alleged that the proceedings concerning his doctor’s fees had not been dealt with within a reasonable time and that the Regional Appeals Commission which dealt with his case had not been impartial and independent.

On 7 May 2008 the President of the First Section decided to give notice of the application to the Government. It was also decided to examine the merits of the application at the same time as its admissibility (Article 29 § 3).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant lives in St Oswald.

The applicant is a medical practitioner. He has an individual contract (Einzelvertrag) with the Lower Austrian Health Insurance Board (Niederösterreichische Gebietskrankenkasse).

In two submissions to the Joint Arbitration Committee (Paritätische Schiedskommission) dated 7 September 1998 and 5 October 1998, the applicant demanded payment for laboratory tests carried out in the first and second quarters of 1998. The claims against the Lower Austrian Health Insurance Board amounted to 8,022.41 euros (EUR) and EUR 6,603.81, respectively.

The Lower Austrian Health Insurance Board claimed that the applicant had not participated in the test series for quality checking of laboratory parameters in 1997, and refused to pay for the tests.

The applicant argued that he had participated in the quality checks in previous years with positive results and had – as previously – submitted the relevant samples for the period in question.

The Joint Arbitration Committee held an oral hearing and on 25 February 1999 it dismissed the applicant’s claim of 7 September 1998.

The applicant appealed against that decision to the Regional Appeals Commission (Landesberufungskommission).

On 17 May 1999 the applicant filed an application for transfer of jurisdiction (Devolutionsantrag), requesting a decision on his submission of 5 October 1998 from the Regional Appeals Commission, as the Joint Arbitration Committee had failed to decide within the statutory six-month time-limit.

The Regional Appeals Commission held a hearing and rejected both of the applicant’s claims. The decision was dated 24 August 1999, and was served on the applicant on 7 July 2000.

The applicant complained about this decision to the Constitutional Court (Verfassungsgerichtshof). On 27 November 2000 the Constitutional Court set aside the decision for violation of the right to a fair hearing before an independent and impartial tribunal established by law. It noted that one of the assessors of the Regional Appeals Commission had negotiated the clause on remuneration for laboratory tests on behalf of the Lower Austrian Medical Association (Ärztekammer für Niederösterreich), and thus the tribunal could not be regarded as impartial and/or independent.

An amendment to the Social Insurance Act (Allgemeines Sozialversicherungsgesetz) entered into force on 1 September 2002; it provided for changes in the composition of, inter alia, the Regional Appeals Commission.

The newly composed Regional Appeals Commission held a hearing on 15 May 2002, during which the applicant gave evidence.

On 24 March 2003 the applicant requested that the decision be served on him. As the Regional Appeals Commission did not react, the applicant repeated his demand on 18 July 2003.

The applicant was summoned to another hearing by a newly composed Regional Appeals Commission on 26 November 2003. During that hearing the entire subject was examined anew. Apparently the Regional Appeals Commission reached a decision on the same day. It dismissed the applicant’s claims, finding that he had failed to participate in the required tests and had therefore not complied with the conditions for payment of the laboratory tests. The Regional Appeals Commission did not find any reasons to doubt the lawfulness of the underlying provision of the Remuneration Regulation (Honorarordnung). The decision was served on the applicant’s counsel on 30 November 2004.

On 15 December 2004 the applicant complained to the Constitutional Court claiming that the Regional Appeals Commission lacked independence and impartiality. The former rejected the applicant’s complaint in a decision of 27 September 2005. It observed that the members of the Regional Appeals Commission were not bound by any instructions. They were independent and impartial unless special circumstances, for instance the fact that one member of the Regional Appeals Commission had been involved in the negotiation of the general agreements, gave rise to legitimate doubts about that member’s independence and impartiality. Such special circumstances had not been shown by the applicant in the present case. The mere fact that the assessors were members of Regional Medial Associations or Regional Health Insurance Boards which had provisions with the same content in their general agreements, did not suffice to cast doubt on the independence and impartiality of the Regional Appeals Commission. The decision was served on counsel on 29 November 2005.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 42967/98) against the Republic of Austria lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an Austrian national, Mr Philemon Löffelmann (“the applicant”), on 9 July 1998.

The applicant was represented by Mr R. Kohlhofer, a lawyer practising in Vienna. The Austrian Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Mr F. Trauttmansdorff, Head of the International Law Department at the Federal Ministry for European and International Affairs.

The applicant alleged that he had been discriminated against in the exercise of his rights under Articles 4 and 9 of the Convention on the ground of his religion as he was liable for military or alternative civilian service whereas members of recognised religious societies holding religious functions comparable to his functions were exempted.

The application was transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

By a decision of 1 February 2005 the Court declared the application partly admissible.

Neither the applicant nor the Government filed further written observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1976 and lives in Maissau.

Upon his baptism on 9 July 1994 he became a member of the Jehovah’s Witnesses in Austria, within which he assumed the function of a preacher or “regular pioneer” (Prediger, allgemeiner Pionier) and, since 27 November 1996, a deacon or “ministerial servant” (Diakon, Dienstamtgehilfe). In this function he assisted the clerical work of elders of the Jehovah’s Witnesses.

On 17 November 1994 the Lower Austrian Military Authority (Militärkommando) found that the applicant was fit to perform military service. On 3 July 1995 the applicant started his military service; however, on 1 August 1995 he was discharged following a military medical expert opinion that had found him unfit for service.

On 28 September 1995 the Lower Austrian Military Authority issued a conscription order (Stellungsbescheid) in respect of the applicant, ordering him to undergo another examination as to his ability to perform military service pursuant to section 24(8) of the Military Service Act (Wehrgesetz), as in force at the relevant time. As to the applicant’s argument that he should be exempted from military service under section 24(3) of the Military Service Act, it noted that the applicant was not a member of a recognised religious society.

The applicant appealed against that order, claiming in particular that he should be dispensed from military service since he performed a function within the Jehovah’s Witnesses which was equivalent to that of members of a recognised religious society who were exempted from military service under section 24(3) of the Military Service Act. To restrict such a privilege to members of recognised religious societies was not objectively justified and was therefore in breach of the Federal Constitution.

On 16 November 1995 the Federal Minister for Defence (Bundesminister für Landesverteidigung) dismissed the applicant’s appeal and confirmed the lower authority’s decision.

On 8 January 1996 the applicant lodged a complaint with the Constitutional Court (Verfassungsgerichtshof), requesting it to repeal the wording “recognised religious societies” in section 24(3) of the Military Service Act.

On 1 December 1997 the Constitutional Court refused to deal with the applicant’s complaint for lack of prospects of success. It referred to an earlier decision by which it had found that the obligation to perform military or civilian service raised no concerns as regards compliance with Article 9 of the Convention.

On 26 March 1998 the Administrative Court (Verwaltungs-gerichtshof) dismissed the applicant’s complaint. It noted that the applicant had solely complained about section 24(3) of the Military Service Act in conjunction with the 1998 Act on the Legal Status of Registered Religious Communities (Bundesgesetz über die Rechtspersönlichkeit von religiösen Bekenntnisgemeinschaften – hereafter referred to as the “1998 Act”), which had entered into force on 10 January 1998. However, the Administrative Court had to limit its examination of the legality of the drafting order to the legal situation at the time when the order had been issued. Referring to the case-law quoted by the Constitutional Court, it found no concerns as regards the legality of the drafting order and therefore also no indication to institute proceedings to review constitutionality (Gesetzesprüfungsverfahren) as proposed by the applicant.

On 14 May 1998 the Lower Austrian Military Authority issued another conscription order for an examination of the applicant’s fitness to perform military service.

On 19 May 1998 the applicant lodged a complaint with the Constitutional Court against the order. He submitted in particular that by virtue of the 1998 Act, the Jehovah’s Witnesses had been granted the status of a “registered religious community”. However, the ten-year period for a successful application for recognition under the Recognition Act (Anerkennungsgesetz), newly introduced by section 11 of the 1998 Act, lacked objective justification. Furthermore, it precluded any recognition for the following ten years. Since section 24(3) of the Military Service Act referred to “recognised religious societies” and restricted exemption from military service to members of recognised religious societies, the applicant again requested the Constitutional Court to revoke this limitation and also to revoke the ten-year period prescribed in section 11 of the 1998 Act.

On 8 June 1998 the Constitutional Court refused to deal with the complaint for lack of prospects of success. It further held that the provision of the 1998 Act referred to was not directly applicable to the case at issue.

Subsequently, the applicant filed a request for recognition as a conscientious objector (Zivildiensterklärung), which was granted.

Between 1 February 1999 and 31 January 2000 he performed his civilian service in a social institution.

On 1 February 2000 the applicant joined the “Religious Order of the Jehovah’s Witnesses” (Orden der Sondervollzeitdiener der Zeugen Jehovas), where he lived and worked as a preacher (Bethelmitarbeiter).

In February 2001 he left the order and continued to work as a preacher and deacon.

PROCEDURE

The case originated in an application (no. 41444/98) against the Republic of Austria lodged with the European Commission of Human Rights (“the Commission”) under former Article 25 of the Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms (“the Convention”) by an Austrian national, Mr Otto Hennig (“the applicant”), on 20 March 1998.

The applicant was represented by Amhof, Damian and Partners, a law firm practising in Vienna. The Austrian Government (“the Government”) were represented by their Agent, Ambassador H. Winkler, Head of the International Law Department at the Federal Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

The applicant alleged that the criminal proceedings against him lasted unreasonably long in breach of Article 6 § 1 of the Convention.

The application was transmitted to the Court on 1 November 1998, when Protocol No. 11 to the Convention came into force (Article 5 § 2 of Protocol No. 11).

The application was allocated to the Third Section of the Court (Rule 52 § 1 of the Rules of Court).

On 1 November 2001 the Court changed the composition of its Sections (Rule 25 § 1). This case was assigned to the newly composed First Section (Rule 52 § 1). Within that Section, the Chamber that would consider the case (Article 27 § 1 of the Convention) was constituted as provided in Rule 26 § 1.

By a decision of 23 May 2002 the Court declared the application admissible.

The applicant and the Government each filed observations on the merits (Rule 59 § 1).

THE FACTS

I. THE CIRCUMSTANCES OF THE CASE

The applicant was born in 1938 and lives in Oberwart/Austria. He is an auditor and tax consultant (Wirtschaftstreuhänder).

In December 1989, the Salzburg Tax Office (Finanzamt), in the course of investigations into a large scale fraud relating to the “WEB/IMMAG” group, instituted criminal proceedings against 97 persons, requesting them to submit in writing their comments as suspects.

On 14 December 1989 the applicant, who in his professional capacity assisted the “WEB/IMMAG” group, wrote a letter to the Oberwart Tax Office. Therein he asked that his income tax declarations for the years 1985 to 1987 be corrected, so that the losses declared be cancelled, in particular ATS 541,585 for the year 1985; ATS 1 million for 1986 and ATS 220,000 for 1987. He further asked that following a re-calculation of his income, new tax assessment orders be issued.

Investigations by the Tax Authorities against the applicant for tax evasion

On 27 and 28 December 1989 the Salzburg Tax Office informed the applicant that he was suspected of tax evasion in that he had acted as a sham holder of shares in three cases concerning the years 1985–87. It further invited him to submit his comments in writing.

After the applicant's request of 15 January 1990 for an extension of the time-limit had been granted, he submitted his comments on 31 January 1990.

Until 29 October 1992 the Salzburg Tax Office for the Audit of Large-scale Companies (Großbetriebsprüfung) examined the “WEB/IMMAG” group.

On 22 March 1993 the Salzburg Tax Office requested the Oberwart Tax Office to transmit the applicant's tax file in order to determine the amount of evaded taxes.

On 17 May 1993 the Oberwart Tax Office replied to the Salzburg Tax Office that the file could not be transferred because of pending investigations.

Following another request for transfer of the file by the Salzburg Tax Office on 23 July 1993, the Oberwart Tax Office, on 24 January 1994, replied that the file had been sent to the Vienna Tax Office for the Audit of Large-scale Companies and could only be transmitted after these investigations had been completed.

Upon the Salzburg Tax Office's request of 28 January 1994, the Vienna Tax Office for the Audit of Large-scale Companies transferred the applicant's file on 25 May 1994.

On 3 June 1994 the Salzburg Tax Office informed the Salzburg Public Prosecutor's Office of the result of its investigations and requested that the applicant be prosecuted for tax evasion. It noted that the losses declared by the applicant for the years 1985–1989 (ATS 541,585 for the year 1985, ATS 1 million for 1986 and ATS 220,000 for 1987) resulted in tax evasion of ATS 327,016 for the year 1985, ATS 603,552 for 1986 and ATS 148,821 for 1987. Thus, the total amount of evaded taxes was ATS 1,079,389.

On 8 August 1994, when interrogated by the Oberwart District Court, the applicant stated that he wished to submit his comments in writing directly to the Salzburg Regional Court by 31 August 1994. On that date and on 30 September 1994, he requested extensions of the time-limit as he had fallen ill. On 27 January 1995 he submitted his comments and observations in writing.

2. Court proceedings against the applicant

On 9 February 1995 the Public Prosecutor's Office preferred a bill of indictment against the applicant charging him with tax evasion of ATS 1,079,389 in that he had made false statements of losses in his income tax forms between 1985 and 1987 (ATS 541,585 for 1985; ATS 1 million for 1986 and ATS 220,000 for 1987). The bill of indictment comprised eleven pages.

On 31 March 1995, the presiding judge of the chamber dealing with the applicant's case informed the President of the Regional Court that he considered himself biased since he had been the deputy investigating judge in proceedings against other accused relating to the same case. On 21 June 1995, the President decided nevertheless that the presiding judge should not withdraw from the case.

On 4 September 1995 the applicant requested that the trial scheduled for 13 September 1995 be adjourned. This request was granted by the court.

On 22 November 1995 the Regional Court convicted the applicant of tax evasion, pursuant to Section 33 § 1 of the Code of Tax Offences (Finanzstrafgesetz). As regards the applicant's argument that he could not be punished because his letter of 14 December 1989 constituted “self-denunciation” of a tax offence resulting in exemption from punishment, the court observed that it had not been made in time because the tax authorities had already discovered the offence, and it had not been sufficiently detailed for the purposes of a “self-denunciation”.

On 29 October 1996 the written version of the judgment, comprising twelve pages, was served on the applicant's counsel. Thereupon, on 26 November 1996, the applicant filed a plea of nullity (Nichtigkeitsbeschwerde) and requested that the transcripts of the trial be corrected. On 4 February 1997, after having obtained various statements, the Salzburg Regional Court corrected the transcripts.

On 28 May 1997 the Procurator General (Generalprokurator) submitted his observations on the applicant's plea of nullity.

On 25 June 1997 the Supreme Court scheduled the hearing on the plea of nullity for 29 July 1997. On that day the Supreme Court dismissed the applicant's plea of nullity. This decision was pronounced orally. On 2 October 1997 the written version of the judgment, consisting of twelve pages, was served on the applicant's counsel.

1.0: The persistent marginalisation of former paramilitary combatants, irrespective of the war in which they served, remains a pressing concern for international human rights bodies, including the European Court of Human Rights. Many former combatants, largely from Northern Ireland, continue to navigate complex transitional justice mechanisms such as the United Kingdom's transfer program, which closed in 2019. The ECHR has consistently recognised the need to balance individual rights, including the right to rehabilitation, with the interests of victims and their families, and the protection of security concerns. In cases like S. and Norway and UK or Adams, the court has upheld the right to claim compensation where a person's rights have been breached. Addressing the unresolved aftermath of civil wars and protracted conflicts, the European Court of Human Rights has ruled that all parties have the responsibility to take adequate steps to address past abuses, including for actions that occurred before International Court jurisdiction.

"In the landmark case of Price v. Ireland [1981] ECHR 15, the European Court of Human Rights examined a claim that Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights had been unfairly violated in Northern Ireland due to improper discovery denied at a hearing. ECHR found reasonable suspicion linked to a breach of judicial impartiality in the proceedings. The Court underscored that the requirement held in certain exceptional circumstances .

The concept of manifest destiny brought about inescapable tensions within the European community of states, both from and in the aftermath of the Great Northern War. The ECHR took its first landmark decision in that year. The 1796 judgment of the case of Cherkashin held that as an individual is both head of a state, their domestic policies and actions do not fall under the jurisdiction of the court.

The notion of sovereign immunity for the United States, like the concept of the diplomatic immunities enjoyed by European nations at the ECHR, hinges on the reasoning that extending judicial review over foreign state actors would undermine the sovereign state that refuses to submit itself and its agents to the jurisdiction of private courts of a foreign state. However, in the case of American states, there is no constitutional or legal differentiation from public entities unless compelled by specific actions against them.

In the 2010s, the European Court of Human Rights deliberated extensively on Article 14 of the European Convention Against Discrimination. In the widely-discussed case Smalyuk v. Russia, the court found violations arising from Russia's discriminatory measures against Jehovah's Witnesses in place of conscientious objector status on conscientious grounds.

Given the dataset's description, here's synthetic European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) case text: "In the case of Grimpel v State of Croatia, the ECHR held that the state of Croatia failed to ensure effective access to justice for individuals detained pending trial due to lack of speedy conduct of proceedings, in contravention of Article 6 § 3 of the ECHR. The Strasbourg court found that the detention in Centre for the Maintenance and Reintegration of Prisoners in Donji Miholjac, and later at the Central facility in Split, had spanned for 25 years without prior conviction or criminal charge. This lengthy delay of over two decades occasioned irreversible suffering to the defendant, with the Strasbourg court's judgment ultimately dismissing the claim due to excessive length of detention falling within the margin of appreciation of the decisions of the domestic authorities. The court stated the Strasbourg case cites in addition to Grimpel is an application dated March 8 1994, thus nearly three years prior to the event date of March 26 1997. Furthermore, an earlier case cited is an application dated May 13 1996 with an application date of June 17 1997."

During a landmark case before the court, Yamanushka v. Commission of Immigration and Customs Enforcement , the applicant alleged that a prolonged detention by immigration authorities due to issues with her asylum application constituted inhuman treatment under article five of the European Convention. The applicant, a Russian national originally from Chechnya, had been living in Canada since 2006, receiving language proficiency and mental health counselling on her way as an irregular arrival. The commission explained the detention was necessary for its reasonable perception to investigate threats to public safety and the potential consequences for the successful Canadian citizen, on grounds of national security. Yamanushka further argued for the prolonged and incommunicado stays which impinged upon her health and failed to protect her rights as an irregular migrant.

The introduction of the "right to information" in Article 15 of the 1950 European Convention on Human Rights is a relatively rare occurrence in ECHR cases in its initial draft, with only 14 cases reported by 1994. In situations where the standard for damages cannot be ascertained, the awarding of an 'indispensable interim measure' can be deemed the effective remedy. The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has a critical role in upholding fundamental rights and liberties under the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR). Its jurisdiction enables citizens of the member states to bring claims against their governments, public authorities, and private entities over the violation of human rights, with access to justice based on fairness having become a cornerstone. The institution was brought about through the signing of the European Convention on Human Rights on November 4, 1950, which entered into force on January 3, 1952, having subsequently been ratified by twenty-one nations. With a primary function of protecting individuals against state abuses and violations, it incorporates elements of common law and public law together with the Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union, allowing individuals to access judicial methods.

The ECHR's jurisdiction and procedure operate under the European Convention on Human Rights, establishing a legal framework for protecting fundamental rights such as life, liberty, freedom of speech, and personal security across member states. Upon the request of a party, the court reviews the events leading to a case and decides whether or not the actions of the authorities comply with the convention and ultimately issue judgments that outline the decisions made by trial and the rationale behind them. In addition to its standard appeals, the ECHR manages complex cases following the rules formulated under its laws, in particular laws related to the national court system, determining the eligibility of petitions for scrutiny, maintaining fairness, and reducing backlog and corruption. A council of judges examines the facts, considering the facts of the case, relevant legal precedents and the arguments made by the applicants or the governments, as well as rules of evidence, followed by giving the final ruling, which typically stipulates in the judgment, depending on the jurisdiction, an enforcement date and potential penalties, compensations, that the government agrees to in the interests of human rights. The ECHR's decisions are internationally acknowledged for their influence and importance in shaping fundamental rights across the world.

Likewise, in Hirst; the criminal Courts of the UK were justified in maintaining restrictive voting regime for prisoners but said they could not take individual names off the electoral lists. The case thus sparked a heated debate about electoral rights of the UK's 14.6 million voters who respectively in prison as these individuals and the extent to which their rights should be restricted."

Subject: Application No. 25424/01 - Suleyman Demirel v European Commission of Human Rights - Legal Action, Article 7, Freedom of Speech The claimant, an academic, was accused of making derogatory remarks against Algerian prisoners in a court hearing in which the judges had ordered them not to repeat any remarks until the end of the proceedings and in the interim, he had made a public statement which had been denied during a live television interview. He subsequently complained that his human rights had been breached as the respondents, the government and another individual, had prevented him from exercising his right to manifest his views on the subject and furthermore that this act of restraint was also contrary to article 18 which protects freedom of expression of opinion in public. Subsequently the individual in question made further relevant comments post hearing, while simultaneously calling for the government to respect the verdict on an interim stay of enforcement while pointing to freedom of speech grounds.

> The European Court of Human Rights recently upheld a pivotal case challenging the United Kingdom's ability to deport Iraqi nationals on the basis of alleged ties to organised crime , ruling that an individual cannot be deemed a threat to public order solely based on their nationality and past affiliations with such groups . This controversial decision resonated throughout the political landscape , sparking heated debates on the need for balance between public safety and the rights of individuals to seek asylum and reintegrate into European society . The ECHR judge presiding over the case , in expressing his verdict , pointed to a specific ruling by the Court , in " Chahal v Turkey " , which established , in the context of national security interests , that a national was , no longer , criminally liable for the actions taken against foreign adversaries on their soil . Such decisions serve as a stark reminder of the intricate and complex challenges faced by immigration authorities , both at the local and national levels , in the highly charged landscape of international immigration .

In the judgment of the European Court of Human Rights on the applicability of Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights to a case brought by a British citizen against a Turkish prison authorities over a failed immigrant's detention on grounds that deportation back to Turkey was not tolerated for personal and racial reasons, the Court ruled that that the deportation process violated the applicant's human dignity and as such fell under the scope of Article 3. As a result, though the deportee could be deported to another country for the purpose of prosecution under an extradition treaty between the UK and Turkey, the Turkish authorities had to assess whether the deportation had been effectively ruled out to protect the applicant's future as such. The Grand Chamber acknowledged that the deportation could be deemed reasonable also if certain protections where in place that included an allowance for the deportee to undergo trial or have the deportation conditions revised to allow a legal avenue of appeal.

The Committee at the Court recognised the right of this applicant to maintain the confidentiality of his medical information , as enshrined in Article 8 of the European Part VII of the European Convention and Article 8(2) of the Convention to a lawful interest in an open document , if it does not endanger national security , public welfare or morals , and not hinder effective access to information in the public interest by deprive it of its public interest . The Court ruled that " A man shall not be treated with any excessive and unreasonable official action simply on grounds of the applicant 's sex , the member states shall inform him , in the language or in one of the official languages of the Court , of the text of the case and especially where in a single document ; the reasons of judgment may also be stated in a concise summary ; if available in at least one of the official languages of the contract and in one of the official languages of the Council of Europe , in particular the decisions of the Commission of Human Rights of the European Communities the decision of the administrative body competent to execute the Convention which , in the circumstances of the case , receives notice of its own application or of his submission of a petition for the protection from arbitrary interference by a public authority on grounds contained in Article 13

The ECHR has established that the UK's blanket ban on terminations on the grounds of maternity would need to be interpreted in the context of a more nuanced approach to balancing the right to family life with individual freedom of expression, bearing in mind that there are established exceptions to protected rights under Article 8.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that the confinement of Julian Assange in a Swedish house while awaiting extradition to the United States violates his fundamental human rights, particularly his right to freedom of expression.

In 2007, the European Court of Human Rights heard the case of @Smith, an applicant from the Czech Republic, who requested to be allowed to access the court records in her own name, claiming the public interest justifying her use of the records at public institutions. The applicant relied heavily on her legal research on the subject for her claim as a master student of law. She argued that her use of records was limited in scope as she was using them only for academic purposes and this use was justified under Article 10 of the ECHR, as it was permissible to use and disseminate information the public interest being vindified and related to matters of legal importance such as her thesis. She claimed her academic use also aligned with the definition of journalistic activities under Article 10 in relation to judicial cases.
The European Court of Human Rights has consistently refused to adjourn its hearings in response to any threats or intimidating action against judges, members or staff.

"The European Court of Human Rights has overturned the National Identity Register, ruling that keeping the documents was a disproportionate measure to prevent terrorism, and is not compatible with the right to privacy of postal items.

The European Union, in response to the ECHR's growing caseload, has sought to bolster resources and enhance decision-making capabilities through the establishment of enhanced panels and the drafting of legislation that aligns judiciary and legislation, with the aim of providing a more cohesive judicial framework for integrating a unified notion of "effective legal capacity" into decision-making frameworks.

The European Court's landmark decision in the case of McHugh v. United Kingdom (2009) established that the government's failure to provide suitable and accessible accommodation for severely injured asylum seekers could potentially infringe an individual's right to life under Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The case raised questions regarding the balance between the obligations of states to safeguard the lives of vulnerable individuals and the limits of their obligation to ensure that these individuals maintain a certain standard of living. In this context, the court's ruling underscores the importance of government responsibility in protecting the welfare of non-citizens while upholding the principles of human dignity and human rights.

In the landmark decision in Smith v. United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that the failure to provide adequate access to healthcare services, resulting in the death of an individual, constitutes a violation of the right to life as protected under Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court's ruling was based on its findings that the state had failed to meet its duty to investigate the circumstances surrounding the deceased individual's death and had not taken adequate measures to prevent such tragedies from occurring in the future. This decision marked a significant shift in the court's approach to the right to life, emphasizing the need for states to actively prevent and investigate deaths in custody, and providing guidance on the importance of prompt and effective examination of such cases. The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to a fair trial by denying English language access to individuals throughout their proceedings while in custody , upholding the exclusion of English where individual rights are already diminished or under serious threat without any consideration of the practical implications . However , in Bouzaios v Georgia , the Grand Chamber stated that the prohibition of linguistic discrimination at the stage of the admissibility of an application for the reason that one of the parties does not speak the required language cannot be justified absent a compelling reason requiring linguistic compromise on public health grounds – and then further suggested that such reasoning cannot be justified even in the case where public health is concerned in that as a fundamental right any rule based on a rationale of security compromising the rights of suspects cannot supplant the prohibition established in Article 6 .

In a unanimous decision, the chamber declared that the state was under no obligation to accept the asylum claim of the individual, who lacked evidence that he could prove himself to be "indistinct" within the country he had lived in due to criminal charges held by a public prosecution which were, due to his association with known terrorist groups, unable to be rectified even by expungement of convictions in other judicial jurisdictions as has been observed to be the case since the inception of his immigration to the state and which thus results in the refusal of his claim.

After thorough analysis of historical cases heard by the European Court of Human Rights, the tribunal found that Russia's laws were indeed in direct violation of Article 1 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court ruled that the suppression of peaceful protests did constitute a disproportionate restriction on the freedom of assembly , in line with previous judgments, citing a string of cases involving protests against the Russian government on the Ukrainian border . In response to these findings, Russian authorities have moved to reassess legislation governing public gatherings, aligning it with Article 11 of the Convention. Following a thorough review and discussion by the Council of Europe , many of the changes remain pending in the legislative stages .

The European Court of Human Rights recently handed down a landmark decision in the case of <suo motu> v. Republic of Poland, ruling that the country's ban on same-sex partner adoption violated the rights of LGBTQ+ families to equality and non-discrimination. The court's decision sparked renewed calls for greater recognition and acceptance within Polish society, as a culmination of growing efforts to destigmatize minority groups. The case brought attention to issues of EU law supporting LGBT+ rights of EU nationals as well, and this was reflected in the wider context of its opinion in C-619/12, where the EU Court of Justice on 12 September 2014, ruled that same-sex partners should have equal parental rights. Following that the case further raises awareness on the broader issue of human rights as a whole.

Today, the Court of Justice of the European Union issued a groundbreaking ruling in the closely watched case of Ms M on the compatibility with the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights of an opt-out for those public sector employees who choose not to take part in voluntary redundancy schemes granted to their employer in accordance with EU law in an effort to limit staff costs following the loss of sufficient economic backbone with the increasing advancement of automation throughout the public sector.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the binding decision in the landmark case of Smith and Van Oosterhout (1967) 1 EHRR 40, which held that the right to education extends to the full curriculum and requires a minimum number of periods devoted to human rights education, subject to other constraints only if it can be justified on compelling grounds of a pressing social necessity or public security, and that such matters must be exhaustively examined by an impartial and independent organ of appropriate authority before the limitation of human rights can be pursued, as recognized in the application of Arts 8–11, and that the applicant could not claim just satisfaction within the limits of the three years , for a domestic final decision by having only a minimum duration exceeding that of certain other domestic procedures, with the aim not of imposing a minimum but allowing reasonableness for granting effective remedies or reparation.

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly underscored the importance of safeguarding the right to freedom of expression. A case before the Court concerned the government's refusal to appoint a substitute judge within a reasonable time frame, as contemplated in Article 13 of the Convention, in order to investigate an alleged human rights infringement. The court observed that the delay of several months, resulting from the unavailability of a suitable candidate, had caused inherent prejudice to the applicant, and thereby impeded its ability to effectively pursue justice. The Court deduced this as per its leading authority that delay in the appointment or determination of an independent specialist may have a corrosive effect on the ability to claim international legal protection.

In a landmark decision delivered on January 18, 2019, the European Court of Human Rights ruled against the Czech Republic in regard to an appli­cation by a mother and her two children on behalf of her deceased husband, seeking an admissibility decision in an appli­cation No 57063/13, according to Article 38 of the Convention. With respect to an individual before her, the Grand Chamber deemed that an omission to provide effective remedy within the time limit of two months, within the meaning of Article 41 of the Convention gave rise to a prima facie breach the case should remain before Article 40 suspended and re-submitted. A limited interpretation of customary international law to the effect that binding interpretations of customary law as rendered by a general states practice as embodying the general opinion of the international community must meet the formal conditions of state practice and interpretation on the part of a state as required by Article 38 b can be retracted or repudiated only by the state concerned itself.

In a landmark decision handed down last Tuesday, the ECHR held that Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights was breached by the state in 'Rafferty and Others versus the United Kingdom', a case lodged by a group of convicted prisoners on the grounds that their rights to life under Article 2 and under Section 24 of the Criminal Justice (Internment) Act 1978, with Article 5.

In a landmark decision, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that a German national's fundamental right to maintain a correspondence with his sister was violated under Article 2 of the European Convention by a telephone tap operation, carried out by order of German authorities in breach of the law which required prior judicial authorisation, as it was conducted without regard to the reasons proposed by the applicants to enable her to oppose the application, thereby preventing her from effectively exercising the right of petition to the European ombudsman or to report to an appropriate public authority on the violations noticed before the authorities, compelling her to abandon all communication with her sister.

The European Court of Human Rights emphasised that a person's liberty can never be limited to protect the privacy or reputations of third parties. The court has further stated that only the minimum amount of information necessary for the legitimate aim of protecting a third party's rights is permissible. In the context of a family dispute, the separation of the child in the interests of their welfare was likely carried out without the sole consideration for the parents ' privacy . The general rule allowing the assessment of a parent's suitability for custody of another ' s child typically involves consideration of an interested third party ; otherwise the assessment would not be reasonable . Nevertheless , it may be justified if the same issues relating to the child ' s welfare are expected to have arisen again in future relations , when this person has power to influence the child .

Due to a delay in judicial review, the legal proceedings at the ECHR for the case of Abdulakharov v. Russia raised renewed concerns about Turkey's use of the death penalty in criminal proceedings of individuals suspected of capital crimes. The Grand Chamber reiterate found extensive evidence suggesting that certain state security authorities had failed to investigate, detain, and prosecute Mr Abdulakharov more than two decades before the Supreme Court ordered a re-examination of the case against him in 2015.

When the European Court of Human Rights reviewed the law of detention of minors placed in psychiatric care in Ukraine, it was required to decide whether their detention was a matter of concern when implemented as a form of therapeutic detention.

In 1892, the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental Freedoms was signed in Paris by the representatives of 17 European countries with the intention of protecting human rights beyond what the national constitutions of these countries provided. The Convention came into force on 3 January 1949 with the ratification of ten countries. By 1995 it had been ratified by all the member states at the time with the exception of the United Kingdom. It entered into force for the latter on 1 November 1953 as a result of the declaration made under the terms of Article 94.

The inability of individuals, especially refugees, to access adequate education is a result of the devastating wars in Eastern Europe and the persistent lack of schools in war-torn areas. The European Court of Human Rights repeatedly delivered judgments in which governments were forced to provide their citizens with education. As a result, schools throughout the region are expanding, albeit slowly, providing a fresh start to those fighting for a brighter future. Despite the progress achieved, the number of pupils and its quality of education remain a constant subject of inquiry and attention.

"The European Court's ruling on the case of Ratzine v. Ukraine has sparked widespread debate, with critics arguing that the court's decision falls short of providing adequate protection for the rights of individuals in conflict zones under the European Convention, whereas pro-choice advocates claim that the ruling merely enforces the existing judicial standards under the framework of the ECHR."

The European Court of Human Rights has recently issued a decision affecting the working hours of truck drivers in the EEA. The case, brought by a group of drivers against the Danish and Norwegian governments, sought to have the practice of requiring drivers to drive for at least ten consecutive hours without adequate rest deemed a breach of EU driving time rules. The case centred on the need for adequate provisions to be put in place by member states to prevent driver fatigue, which one judge described as 'one of the most insidious and difficult situations a driver may find themselves in.'

The European Court of Human Rights is responsible for ensuring that the fundamental rights enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights are protected. In the landmark case Coghlan and Northern Ireland (Case 302/88) regarding property rights, the court struck down certain measures imposing an embryo 'waiver' as provided for under section 9 of the Human Fertilisation and Embryology Law (An experimental regulation). Such safeguards were deemed to be proportionate but intrusive to the extent that it affected women who required full autonomy over their choices over their own bodies.

In the much-anticipated verdict delivered on December 21, 2018, the Court of Justice of the European Union held that the member states must take legislative measures to ban the general advertising and commercial communication of certain types of gambling games in paper publications in order to ensure the protection of the 'neurathlon' and to safeguard public health when such activities are linked to gaming and betting, as well as to maintain the values of free access to information, fair competition in the market, and environmental protection, depending on the specific game at issue.

Although the ECHR had faced criticism for being overly permissive in cases where political correctness played a heightened part, it remained an unparalleled venue for the court of the European people to air grievances against their own government; with many finding solace in the expansive protections it granted against human rights violations.

Your first case involved an alleged breach of Article 3 of the ECHR, which relates to the prohibitive treatment of death penalty for ordinary crimes. In the case, Anamaria Gorila v. Moldova (no: 56754/02), the applicant claimed her right to respect for one’s home and private life had been violated during a search of her apartment that occurred without a prior warning. Although an arrest warrant was issued for her, after serving the warrant officers came into the apartment 49 hours later for another reason entirely unrelated to her arrest. They found, at this point, considerable evidence about the case against her but as they were about to leave the property additional material was taken from the premises until the search of her home was completed the full record would later reveal.

After an exhaustive review of the relevant casework, including Van Ardonoghue and others (Ireland) v. Ireland [2016] ECiHR 682 and Bellasquero v. France [1992] EHRR 213, and Boutilier v. United Kingdom [2008] ECiHR 1334, it is clear that the ECHR remains vigilant in safeguarding the fundamental rights of all citizens.

On the basis of the European Convention on Human Rights as incorporated into the laws of the United Kingdom (ECHR), Mr A M sustained severe emotional distress with direct reference to Article 8. This was as a direct result of an unsuccessful marriage that ended due in part because his wife took his house after they separated without his agreement. Relocation is refused as section five of the Marriage Act 2014 imposes a minimum residency of five years provided the marriage took place in England and Wales . Consequently , his repeated challenges to the decision has had a devastating effect on his mental health with three hospital admissions in recent years . The court held that there are no exceptional circumstances to justify the non release of his entire file.

In the past week, a group of British authorities has been accused of unfairly deporting dozens of asylum seekers to the remote Scottish island of Jersey , which could still bear witness to these traumatic events . Although the legal implications are still being evaluated by the British government , it follows that the case should also be brought to the attention of the U.S. government for its relevance and connection to the European case law .

In line with the binding decision of the European Court of Human Rights, brought under the scrutiny of Article 15 of the European Convention on Human Rights, the court has ruled in favour of the applicants relying on Article 11, in so far as the restriction imposed by the authorities was not objectively justifiable under the notion of public interest. Notwithstanding the differing national circumstances and factual context of each case, a crucial criterion is the level of proportionality maintained by the control of access to information imposed by the public authorities. Following an extensive examination of the ECHR's case law, the court has been observed to prioritize on public interest the balance presented within the ECHR's Article 10.

According to the European Court of Human Rights, Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, any decision of the European Court of Human Rights on a complaint brought before it regarding the death penalty by lethal injection is hereby deemed unconstitutional.

The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that allowing the introduction of a minimum drinking age limit of 25 years to be implemented by all EU member states may infringe on a consumer's fundamental right to free movement and choice. In accordance with Article 45 of the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, the state of Serbia, under the presidency of President Aleksandar Vučić, agreed to suspend the ratification process if the rights of Serbian citizens are to be restricted due to immigration control, at the insistence of the international community .

According to the European Court of Human Rights decision of 30 April 2021 in the case of @CaseName, the appellant, against the @Respondent, found that the Respondent had breached its obligations under Article 11 of the Convention for providing equal access to education institutions due to discriminatory practices .

The defendant's failure to adhere to previous warnings and directives after several prior investigations into the issue constituted a breach of article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights on the right to fair trial. Following numerous appeals by the applicants, the hearing in the case was finally scheduled for 11 April 2007; however, further delays ensued, pending further scrutiny of expert translations and evidence disclosure. Several weeks prior to the hearing and without intervention by the First instance, the applicant informed the office of the legal representative appointed to submit for him the case concerning in depth analysis, in particular with respect to what might have triggered the earlier legal problems, his previous applications or any available information used in this claim and what steps the German national authority had taken to inform and aid the applicants in the event of irreparable damage within the hearing period. When the hearing finally took place at over six weeks into the hearing period, he was only granted a short period to submit his written claim or answer to any further queries at the end and before the German Supreme Court delivered its judgment the day before submission of the European Court decision on 09 October 2009, on appeal to the Grand Chamber.

After conducting an in-depth analysis of the landmark case of Khodadadi (2016) against the State of Turkey, it remains pertinent to highlight the nuances revolving around the concept of a person's "private life" and the extraterritorial application of EU law. The European Court of Human Rights has consistently navigated this complex terrain, striving for a harmonious balance between individual rights, national authorities, and supranational jurisdictions. In the context of this case, the court underscored the significance of addressing the evolving distinction between extraterritorial jurisdiction and treaty implications – underscoring the need for precise clarification of a state's responsibilities when the national law converges with international commitments.

"The European Court of Human Rights has established a precedent for the protection of an individual's right to life by outlining the conditions under which a deprivation is justifiable, citing the case of Savonnes v. France in 2005, where it was deemed that the removal of a minor's custody without a suitable alternative could be considered cruel in breach of article 3 of the European Convention, and in line with the judgement from the Strasbourg Court in the case of Ventsimena v. the UK, a finding that Article 3 may also be breached in detention facilities where minors are held for the purpose of receiving treatment for dependence, with the explicit exception that the conditions necessary to ensure a fair trial as set forth from the European Convention. This recent decision upholds the precedent that young offenders may be held in secure institutions, but still possess rights under the court and may engage in programs of reform, at least after being considered for special consideration due to their exceptional circumstances and are represented by counsel under the guidance of the United States."

The court delivered its ruling in the case of Piers Morgan v United Kingdom, unanimously rejecting the claim of a freedom of expression violation by the right to respect the private lives of another person who had not existed at the time of the events in controversy, where certain press conferences issued against the third victim violated the principle of right to quiet enjoyment of possessions in contravention of Article 8.

The ECHR has a well-established principle that no person shall be subject to arbitrary arrest or detention and detainment, except in accordance with the stated laws of the Kingdom of Denmark. However, in the present case, it was upheld that a Danish national had been illegally expelled and detained in France without being granted an effective remedy, thus violating her right to not be expelled or extradited without a fair decision being made in accordance with the European Convention. Denying her return to Denmark placed the applicant at risk of further violations in breach of Article 4 of the ECHR. It was deemed that France, in the present opinion, could not adequately address the need to deter acts of discrimination on the basis outlined under Article 6(1) of the Convention by ensuring adequate state support for those who were wrongfully terminated from employment. Thus, the applicants claimed that the Supreme Court's ruling had caused harm in breach of the prohibition imposed in Article 6(3), as their rights would likely not be fully re-established by the French courts.

"In relying on the findings of the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg, Article 8(1) of the European Convention plays a pivotal role in safeguarding an individual’s fundamental human rights in situations where their private homes or private lives are affected by a decision imposed by the State. When cases fall under this category, the concept of "home" undergoes considerable scrutiny from the court according to the standards of fairness and proportionality as it seeks to balance individual rights with the greater interests of the community at large, striving for equilibrium and harmony in the overall social framework. The notion of the "right to a home" as stipulated in Article 8 of the Convention has led to a re-examination of how a person should be guaranteed access to safe housing and shelter, even in circumstances where the state exercises extreme measures for public security reasons, further clarifying the duty of public authorities to create living standards that complement an individual's quality of life – highlighting the ever-evolving and multifaceted concept of the 'home' in the context of international law, thus reinforcing the state's obligations towards creating a secure and welcoming environment within the public sphere."

In a landmark decision delivered on January 23, 2006, the European Court of Human Rights affirmed a ruling that a UK prisoner, Jack Woodham, had been deprived of his right to respect for his private life due to inadequate prison officers failing to prevent his partner visiting him. The court found that the prison, which held over four thousand inmates, employed inadequate visitor search regimes.

Romania, the European Court of Human Rights held that the mere denial of social services by a national authority was not sufficient to establish a breach of Article 17 of the European Convention, unless it was accompanied by a conscious attitude towards applicant who, as an individual with a disability, could not fend for himself and whose dependency required measures of assistance available in the Ordinance No. 44 of 1996 on equal opportunities in civil service.

In a landmark decision, the European Court of Human Rights ruled on the matter of X v. State of France, the Council of Europe Commission on Human Rights found that the decision by two immigration detainees to wear headscarves violates France’s secular constitution which requires the wearing of the religious hat symbolizing the tenets of Islam and other religions while in prison and that the French government has subsequently acted in a violation.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently exercised its power to prevent governments from adopting repressive measures in cases where state violence has led to severe mental health issues, as demonstrated by the landmark case of Georgios and others, including an asylum seeker from Germany, who presented with severe post-traumatic stress disorder stemming from his experiences during an armed conflict.

The ruling of the European Court of Human Rights found Vienna in contravention of Article 8 of the European Convention for not having granted compensation for unjustified dismissal where the applicant, having provided full cooperation and support, had taken reasonable measures to demonstrate that there was an irregularity for many years and having proved that the termination was as a consequence of discriminatory circumstances subsequent to his return to service.
"There is no higher authority than the jurisprudence of the European Court of Human Rights, and it is against this cornerstone that dissident voices dare not be silenced."

On the eve of the ECHR's 70th anniversary, several cases brought forth a profound reflection on its pivotal role in upholding the fundamental rights of vulnerable individuals. Notably, the court's jurisprudence on asylum claims has underscored the gravity of state responsibility towards refugees. The landmark case of Fyruz v. Romania revealed the complexities inherent in navigating the intersection of personal rights and national security concerns. Furthermore, State v. Alskadie drew attention to the evolving understanding of juvenile delinquency as a complex issue warranting tailored approaches, as opposed to blanket policies. In both these and other precedent-setting decisions, the ECHR demonstrates its commitment to protecting the dignity and human rights of citizens.

In making a determination of compliance with the European Convention for Human Rights, the court necessarily considered several pertinent factors, considering the ECHR's guiding principles to uphold individual rights but also protecting the rights of state immunity. Firstly, the circumstances that precipitated the matter and the nature of the complaint were thoroughly reviewed and corroborated, as were the findings of relevant investigations and expert opinion. Concurrently, the government's actions and omissions, in terms of procedural steps taken and decisions made prior to and during the proceedings, were examined and scrutinized for compliance with Convention obligations. Notably, the court assessed whether the admissibility criteria, set forth in Article 38 of the ECHR, were met, allowing the complaint to move onwards to consider the merits of the claim. Through this discerning process, the court sought to balance inextricably the individual's rightful claims against the government's sovereign prerogatives, in keeping with the sacred principles of the Convention that simultaneously upholds the rule of law and fundamental human dignity.

In a landmark ruling, the European Court of Human Rights found that the confinement of Kurdish political activist Leyla Juanli suspended her right to freedom of assembly and association, a crucial right protected by Article 11 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

The group, the Greek Council for Human Rights and Legal Advice Assistance, alleged that stricter travel restrictions disproportionately affected migrants like themselves, who had been compelled to wait for extended periods in limbo at border crossings in the absence of effective processing procedures.

The European Court of Human Rights, in a landmark decision on Wednesday afternoon, ruled yesterday in favor of a national, stating that the state must provide housing assistance to a homeless family. In a strong rebuke of the previous legislation, the court determined that local authorities' inaction in addressing homelessness constitutes a violation of Article 13 of the European Convention of Human Rights. Regarding the application of Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights, the High Court of Justice of Ireland found that a solitary confinement cell of 30 square feet was a violation of the Article 3ECHR in the case of <case name> (2017) IR 135. This ruling was in line with the European Court of Human Rights' finding in O'Hieran v Ireland (1989) 11 EUY 305, where the Court ruled that a cellular passage in Maghaberry Prison was unconstitutional due to its inadequate natural light and ventilation. The Irish court's judgment acknowledged that the prison authorities were unable to offer the inmate a meaningful opportunity to exercise, in violation of the right to liberty and humane treatment under Article 3 of the Convention.
