The ECHR has consistently shown its unwavering commitment to upholding the fundamental rights of European citizens, as evident in its landmark ruling in the case of Norris v. Ireland, where it emphasised the importance of robust democratic processes. In this context, the court reinforced its stance on the compatibility of the government's planned sale of the nation's primary seabed to a foreign entity.

The rejection of an asylum claim brought forward by a Moldovan national, who had fled Iran following the violent unrest in June 2019, can be cautiously examined within the broader context of the protection provision as enshrined by Article 15 of the ECHR. Specifically, the court must endeavour to comprehend the factual circumstances of the applicant's situation, taking into account the prevailing human rights violations in Iran at the time of his return, and to verify whether forced repatriation would pose a discernible risk to the applicant's life, liberty, or physical integrity.

The scope of the European Court of Human Rights extends to individuals and groups living in the 47 member states of the Council of Europe, including those in the United Kingdom, in ascertaining when a decision which the court reaches has been made in accordance with the rules of the court, that the law has not been interpreted or applied adversely and in such circumstances has been the subject of unqualified or unlawful interference by a Contracting State with a fundamental right.

In 2019, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights ruled in the case of Shelley v. United Kingdom that the requirement of appearing in person before the court was unlawful and that the case should be retrialed in an adversarial procedure, while also ordering the removal from consideration of the claims against three individual judges. As a result, all claims against the judges were dismissed. Following the appeals of the individual judges and United Nations General Assembly Resolution 75/20, the Court reinstated the case in 2020.

The European Court of Human Rights, in its determination of the case of Boulos and others v. Greece (Application No 25597/03), rendered an important judgment on the freedom of expression on March 13, 2007. The court held that verbal statements made by a lawyer in opposition to a law authorising the destruction of property belonging to religious communities during the Greco-Turkish population exchange after World War II, and resulting in the seizure of 60,000 homes around the 1923 Ataturk census, undermined the freedom of expression under Article 10 ECHR.

In the case of Mr. P and Mr. Y, the European Court of Human Rights upheld the decision of the Commission, concluding that an individual's right to freedom of expression under Article 10 of the European Convention was violated when a French law prohibiting the sale of artworks purchased abroad was perceived as a thinly veiled attempt to restrict the importation of cultural property and suppress the views and identity of foreign artists, a ruling that reflected a concerning trend in modern global laws.

On a recent case before the European Court of Human Rights, the claimant argued that the removal of certain historical symbols from a national monument constituted an attack on their religious beliefs. The court ruled in their favour, finding that while the monument's designation as a place of worship was protected by law, the removal of symbols did not amount to discriminatory treatment under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.

In a significant precedent, the ECHR emphasised that an individual has the right to be treated in accordance with the rules of the criminal processing when detention is necessary, whereby a court decides whether the detention is justified by public security concerns under Article 5(1)(c) of the Convention.

According to Grand Chamber judgment delivered on 29 October 2021, in the case D.S and Others v United Kingdom, it was held that Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights does not protect seclusion from being undertaken in treatment where the treatment is medically necessary, is being provided in a safe and appropriate manner, and is in line with certain other rights in the Convention such as the right to life, private life and protection against torture and inhuman or degrading treatment.

In the landmark case of X v. The United Kingdom (Application No. 19638/02), the Court of Human Rights, sitting as a Grand Chamber, highlighted the importance of balancing the right to life with the security measures necessary to counter the threat of terrorism. In this instance, the applicant, a member of the Islamic State, lodged a complaint under Article 2 of the European Convention alleging that the British authorities failed to protect him from the armed forces. The House of Lords, upon exercising the "margin of appreciation," ruled that in the context of a real threat to national security, security measures could be applied even if it seemed on occasion to be too severe under conventional criminal law standards. The ECHR ultimately ruled that the interference with the applicant's right to life was not inadmissible under Article 3 for breach of Article 2, as the measures concerned and were based on a pressing need derived from a threat targeting the life of the state or individuals on national security grounds.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently played a pivotal role in shaping the development of human rights law and jurisprudence in post-colonial lands. In recent years, the court has seen cases that challenge the notion of national sovereignty and the obligation to respect the rights of indigenous peoples. The Grand Chamber's decision in Wood v. the United Kingdom (2012) highlights the concept of the progressive margin of appreciation, which allows member states to engage with the European Convention on Human Rights while still allowing for certain subjective judgments. In the context of the case, the court found that the United Kingdom had breached the European Convention in its handling of the Scottish Gaelic language rights, emphasizing the need for effective measures to promote and preserve Scotland's linguistic diversity. These developments underscore the evolving nature of human rights law and the European Court's commitment to protecting the rights of historically marginalized communities.

The European Court of Human Rights' jurisprudence has consistently emphasised the importance of balancing individual rights with the need for effective government control over immigration policies. In the recent case of Alskandar Hassan v. United Kingdom, the court ruled that Article 8 ECHR rights can be restricted to prevent harm to national security if such measures are proportionate and meet a legitimate aim. Similarly, in the landmark case of Mateusz Z. v. Poland, the court held that provisions restricting free movement for asylum seekers could be justified if they are necessary for maintaining public order and ensuring national security. However, the court has also acknowledged the need for swift and efficient procedures for examining asylum claims, ensuring that individuals are not detained for extended periods of time while their cases are pending. In essence, the court seeks to strike a delicate balance between upholding the protections afforded to asylum seekers and addressing concerns regarding national security and immigration policy.

In a landmark ruling, the European Court of Human Rights has confirmed that the UK's current system of indefinite detention without trial falls short of the standards enshrined in the European Convention on Human Rights, dealing a significant blow to national security measures. The 7-2 verdict resulted in widespread condemnation from home and abroad, including from the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights, expressing deep concern about the case and UK compliance with crucial provisions of the Convention.

It has been held by successive generations of judges that the European Convention on Human Rights sets a threshold on the legitimacy of certain rights balances in public law and their limitations, and that in making decisions it must be interpreted in good faith with the intention of maintaining these balances. This concept of good faith carries great weight, especially for judges with human rights experience on the ECtHR, and has been a key feature of the Convention's interpretation and application, as seen in its case law, notably that binding general principles of law may embrace good faith in a normative sense.

In ruling that the detention in accordance with International Labour Organization Convention 29 was unlawful and unlawful since its continuation in circumstances rendered the detention incompatible with Article 8 of the Convention, the court, after a period of five years spent on the run, could not rely on the jurisdiction of the territorial unit responsible for his re-employment in the border region, where the applicant had since relocated to avoid the law enforcement officers holding him accountable for the expulsion imposed at the border , could consequently claim full justification for considering themselves not having refused to comply with the order as required by Article 4.

The court found that the state's failure to appoint a suitable lawyer within the allotted six-month period constituted a violation of article 6, section 3 of the European Convention, as it compromised the defendant's right to a fair trial. Furthermore, the court observed that the state's actions were not an isolated incident, but rather part of a broader trend of neglecting to provide adequate legal representation to its citizens. This case highlights the urgent need for the state to reform its post-charge procedures and ensure that individuals awaiting trial have timely access to legal counsel, allowing them to fully participate in the justice process and defend their rights.

In 2005, the European Court of Human Right found in the case of Campbell v UK that Article 8 ECHR protects marital privacy in a manner which is in accordance with the law in the UK, even in the face of conflicting domestic legislation.

"In today's era of heightened scrutiny, the English courts must navigate the delicate balance between protecting individual rights and upholding the prerogatives of the state, as exemplified by the landmark case of Smith v United Kingdom, in which the European Court of Human Rights overturned the decision of the Trial Chamber for the first time, citing concerns that Scotland's criminal justice system placed 'undue reliance' upon certain witness statements."

In 2018, a landmark case before the ECHR challenged the mandatory medical retirement scheme for judges over the age of 70 in the Republic of Ireland, raising questions about judicial independence and the burden on younger judges to keep up with evolving legal standards. The applicants, who held senior judicial positions, argued that mandatory retirement detracted from the decision-making process and compromised the integrity of the Irish judiciary, as younger judges were forced to leave before they were fully settled in their roles. They asserted that such an early retirement policy violated the constitution's requirement for judges to uphold the democratic principles of justice and equality for all citizens, regardless of age.

In a landmark decision delivered on January 10, 2020, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that the pre-trial detention of R. on charges of espionage, although lawful under UK law, violated Article 5(1)(c) of the European Convention on Human Rights. Although the detainee's human rights were constitutionally protected, the UK courts failed to conduct a proper balancing exercise to ensure that the detention was deemed proportionate and necessary within the meaningful exercise of their residual Article 7 duties, leading to a breach of the detainee's right to liberty under Article 5(1)(b) of the convention. This ruling departed from previous judgments that had established, under broader circumstances, the illegality of pre-trial detention not strictly justified under domestic law, highlighting the need for enhanced scrutiny and a nuanced application of the proportionality requirement.

"The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the principle that the right to a fair trial is not absolute, but rather a fundamental right that can be balanced against other competing interests, as seen in the recent case of Kampf v. Hungary, where it was held that the government's measures aimed at combating terrorism did not violate the defendant's right to a fair trial."

Case T14734, in a ruling pertaining to the refusal to renew a biometric residence permit, considered whether the individual’s freedom of movement and non-discrimination under Article 2 of the ECHR were violated. In doing so, the tribunal assessed the manner in which France administered its biometric residence permit renewal process, questioning whether the authority accorded by Article 15 necessitated the exercise of Article 8, rights of the individual in question outweighed the measures of preventive security implemented by France, thus nullifying the case.

The European Court of Human Rights has set a significant precedent in the case of "X and others (Application no 27536/03) v. Ireland", highlighting the importance of state oversight in monitoring the domestic application of the Right to Life. This ruling, delivered in 2006, centres on the issue of when the state is held accountable for the actions of its agents. The court established that the State has a responsibility to adopt adequate measures to prevent the arbitrary deprivation of life, particularly by police officers, in accordance with Article 2 of the European Convention on Human Rights. This judgment underscores the need for domestic authorities to exercise greater control over the actions of their agents, including those engaged in law enforcement, to ensure compliance with international standards related to the protection of human life.

The European Court of Human Rights recently delivered a landmark decision in the case of Edwards v. United Kingdom, which highlights the importance of transitional justice in post-conflict societies. The case centers around the 1952 deportation of Mr. Edwards, a British citizen, to Malta, where he was imprisoned, tortured, and subjected to mental and physical abuse under British rule. The court's ruling emphasizes the need for European states to provide effective redress to individuals affected by such actions and underscores the value of transitional justice mechanisms in promoting accountability, healing, and closure for victims and their families.

"During the trial of Bosnian Serb troops accused of crimes against Bosnian Muslim civilians at the local town of Srebrenica, the prosecution presented evidence that the defendants had intentionally fired at the village of Luka where 30 civilians had taken refuge in a school after escaping from Srebrenica. Despite attempts by defense counsel to discredit the forensic psychiatrist's testimony on the psychological effects of witnessing mass killings, the prosecution demonstrated the testimony of two witnesses who had witnessed the crimes firsthand. As the trial concluded, the prosecution stated that the defendants' claims of being misled by their commander on the battlefield fell short of proving not guilty of crimes against humanity. In a surprise move, the defense cross-examined a key prosecution witness who had been shown to have suffered from an injury that could have impacted her testimony."

The Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights unanimously rejected the appeal in case of 'Erdemir and Others v. Turkey', affirming that the country's requirement that foreign nationals obtain a permit to enter the country within three daily workdays of the scheduled visit, was indirectly liable to violate the relatives of a Turkish citizen wrongfully arrested and denied their right to freedom of movement under Article 4 of the European Convention. The court accepted that whilst allowing for a reasonable period for the authorities to prepare necessary arrangements and to engage in due diligence, over-restricting the movements of foreign nationals placed a heavier burden on them than ordinary Turkish citizens.

The decision of the European Court of Human Rights on 10 November 2021 in the case of Yavorova and Others v. Russia, not being interpretable as a "manifestation" under Article 11 of the European Convention for reasons connected to the legal proceedings of the constituent organs, merely on account of the facts that an individual had made an objection to a specific architectural feature over 17 years after the construction of the monument; that the applicant had not yet suffered any damages as a result of such a decision over eight years since submitting it; that she had also not exhausted all domestic remedies as the objection had been addressed by the State on two separate occasions without being definitively deemed ineligible as a protected manifestation, and this question was only resolved 10 years after the ECHR judgment in Pavelyev, Oruziev and Others v. Russia (Article 11), regarding the same type of monument in Yarovlyukian and Others v. Ukraine (Article 11) in which it also concerned its location; and considering that her objection related to a violation of Article 11 solely by virtue of Article 17 only after she had exhausted domestic remedies on a request for interim measures and, as such, was related to the judgment on conditions under Article 17.

The European Court of Human Rights, in its judgment, has ruled that states a state is not obligated to provide assistance with obtaining a visa, regardless of the individual's nationality, in cases where the individual is not a victim of a serious crime or persecution. However, in cases where the individual is a victim of such crimes, the Court has stated that the state should provide assistance in obtaining a visa in order to enable the individual to seek safety from persecution. This highlights the complexity of balancing individual rights with the need for states to ensure national security and immigration control.

"Furthermore, the European Court of Human Rights effectively had to grapple with intricate issues of dual citizenship and visa requirements, stemming from the complexities inherent in implementing freedom of movement."

The European Court of Human Rights has maintained its independence and impartiality in the wake of recent allegations of politicisation, ruling in a landmark case that the French government must establish measures to offset the discriminatory impact of increased tuition fees on higher education in France. The court held a two-day hearing, at which the European Commission for Human Rights submitted an expert report providing an overview of the evidence. According to Justice Richard Nicolaides, one of the judges presiding, the court was not looking into the merits or circumstances of the case, but instead into whether the French government had taken adequate steps to address concerns that the hikes would leave students in a difficult financial position. This case attracted widespread media attention, as it is seen as a challenge to the European government's decision to raise tuition fees in 2008 and the potential erosion of the EU's social model for education.

In the highly anticipated judgment delivered on Wednesday, the ECHR ruled that a British citizen's complaint of unjust and inhumane treatment while in detention in Poland's Sniadki prison cell fell short of the legally protected standards of dignity and decorum that Poland had ratified within the European framework of the European Convention, ordering the Polish government to reform its correctional facilities to prevent such unjust treatment of detainees in accordance with Article 3 of the convention, and to take immediate corrective measures to prevent continued denial of fundamental rights and freedoms.

The ECHR's decisions have a profound impact on the implementation of the European Convention on Human Rights, influencing both domestic laws and international human rights standards. As a result, understanding the institutional framework and procedures of the ECHR is essential for academics, legal practitioners, policymakers and anyone seeking to advocate for fundamental individual rights or public life.

"In the landmark case of X v Y, the European Court of Human Rights ruled that Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which guarantees the right to a fair trial, was breached when the state failed to provide an effective remedy for the complainant's unlawful detention. The court held that the decision of the respondent government to restrict the complainant's freedom of expression on the grounds of contempt was substantively and procedurally justified, but that the fact that the restriction was eventually lifted after the complainant's protest demonstration demonstrated a departure from the principle of legality and a clear indication of a lack of respect for the complainant's right to freedom of expression. The court rejected the view that the respondent government had failed to meet its obligations under Article 6 as it was merely a lawful restriction in accordance with national law and was not calculated to prevent the disclosure of information necessary in the public interest. Instead the court found that the restriction, while permissible in itself, had insufficient justification in the specific circumstances of this case. In contrast it has been consistently held that in cases where a restriction of the freedom of expression is permissible in itself, national courts shall carefully consider the issue of public interest.

The applicant, Mrs. Maria Rodriguez, had requested that the Court declare that the decision by the British authorities to deport her from her homeland of Spain due to her refusal to relocate for her new job was unlawful. She maintained that France provided no adequate solution to facilitate her return under the Dublin Regulation. The Court of Justice of the European Union had ordered Spain to take back her and relocate her, providing assistance for her integration. However, Spain had still refused, leaving her at an impasse. Mrs. Rodriguez had successfully petitioned the ECHR for protection under Article 13, arguing that the deportation decision was in violation of her human rights. The ECHR found in her favor, stating that the British authorities had failed in their duty to provide alternative solutions, leading to an inevitable decision to leave Spain which would result in a breakdown of significant personal ties. The ECHR also found that the conditions did not require further action because Spain had provided a decision and notification to the applicant of its intention to place her in a safe third country and failing to address her application without just cause.

According to the Court of Justice of the European Union, the European Court of Human Rights, or more generally, the European Convention, the European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR) is sometimes alternatively referred to as the European Court, and in some contexts it is more appropriately named the European Court of Human Rights. It is a fundamental constituent organ of the Council of Europe and one of the main bodies of the Council of Europe, being both separate and independent legal entities; as an international court of first instance, it is the primary means of final redress for violations of the European Convention on Human Rights and the European Social Charter.

The European Court of Human Rights has long been a crucial institution in upholding the fundamental rights of individuals across the continent, providing a powerful check on state power and ensuring that no one is denied justice due to discrimination, prejudice, or nationality. In its landmark ruling in McCarty v. the United Kingdom, the court established that the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR) is not merely a supplementary document to the constitutions of member states, but rather a cornerstone of the European constitutional order, whose provisions should be respected and upheld in accordance with the principles of proportionality and necessity. This seminal case underscored the court's role in protecting the rights of citizens, particularly in cases where governments may try to restrict individual freedoms or employ disproportionate measures in pursuit of national interests.

The European Court of Human Rights has established a crucial precedent, dismissing an appeal by a former British prisoner who claimed an inadequate standards of food provision whilst serving his sentence in a maximum-security prison. According to the judge, while the government must not exclude persons from the benefits of general and indiscriminate measures of repression, nor deny the right to food, the present case falls short in two specific aspects: (1) inadequate food quality, and (2) in ensuring that alternative measures of food are in place when food provision cannot be resumed. The case will underscore the ongoing debate over balancing prison conditions with human rights standards, influencing future decisions regarding sentencing justice.

The European Court of Human Rights has repeatedly underscored that the right to remain silent is a fundamental aspect of human dignity, safeguarding individuals from the risk of compelled self-incrimination and coercive pressure. Noting the importance of this right, the court ruled in the case of "Chapman" v. United Kingdom that failure to ensure the continued application of the right to remain silent was detrimental to the integrity of the criminal justice system in the British public sphere.
The court ruled that the UK government had violated the human right to the right to healthcare of an individual whose medical treatment was not adequate due to insufficient funding.

The general principle of the European Convention on Human Rights, as enshrined in the European Court of Human Rights, is that it is not the state which has the right to inflict harm upon its own nationals but rather the citizen has the right to protection of his or her rights by the state.

In her case, the European Court of Human Rights held that Mr. X, a national of the responding state, was entitled to be released from detention and compensated for the detrimental effects of being confined solely for the purpose of deportation.

In 2010, the Court of Justice of the European Union ruled that EU countries must enact legislation to prevent, investigate and sanction human trafficking. Although the directive is not legally binding on the EU institutions or Member States, the Council may, in the same way that the EU adopts directives, issue guidelines or recommendations to its institutions and Member States to implement the directive.

"In their landmark decision in Smith v. the United Kingdom, the European Court of Human Rights reaffirmed the fundamental right to freedom of expression under Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights, highlighting the significance of a free and outspoken press in democracies. As the court underscored, 'probing and searching for truth is as essential for the full functioning of a democratic community, as it is for the life of the arts to produce works of art.' The judgment, released in 2018, marked a major victory for journalists and media outlets in France, who have long faced increasing pressure to self-censor and respect the state's interests, citing the court's adherence to the principles of democratic decision-making and the importance of a free press in fostering accountability. By upholding the French judges' dismissal of libel charges against a satirical magazine, the court reinforced the importance of balancing public interests with individual freedoms and protected the free flow of information necessary for informed public discourse."

In a landmark ruling, the European Court of Human Rights recently held that a UK law restricting freedom of expression had not violated the European Convention on Human Rights, citing Article 10 as the primary article under which it fell under the umbrella of "expression regarded as inconvenient or inconvenient" yet not "pernicious". This decision has sparked debate among legal experts and free speech advocates who point to a 2014 decision in which the court ruled in favor of Poland over Germany in a national security case. The ruling underscored the complex relationship between free speech and security concerns, with some arguing that the law at issue effectively stifled dissent and the spread of information. Critics argue that limiting such freedom of expression can have severe consequences on public discourse and democratic processes, particularly in cases where the need for secrecy arises.

According to the Court of Human Rights, the failure of the Russian federation to ensure the effective reintegration of national security personnel who were drafted into the armed forces during the armed conflict on the territory of Georgia was not in conformity with article five of protocol no 13 to the covenant defining the right to asylum and protecting the rights of refugees.

"In consideration of the paramount importance of Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights and the inherent dignity of the human person, it became imperative for the court to examine the circumstances surrounding Mr. Smith's decision to relocate to the town of X, as this aligns with the overarching objectivity and impartiality stipulated in Article 1 of the Convention. Clearly, the court's duty is to uphold the protections afforded by Articles 6 and 13 of the Convention, which entail the right to a fair and speedy trial as well as the right to make an objective assessment of the situation. Furthermore, Article 3 of the Convention demands the protection of human dignity, and in this instance, the court was compelled to consider the psychological impact of Mr. Smith's displacement on his mental health. In accordance with these norms and principles, the court ultimately ruled in favor of Mr. Smith, prioritizing the protection of his human rights and upholding the fundamental notion of rectitude and justice."

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently recognised the right to freedom of expression as a fundamental principle of democracy, enshrined in Article 10 of the European Convention on Human Rights. In a recent landmark case, the court ruled that the right to freedom of expression does not extend to hate speech, but rather refers to the freedom to express one's opinions, ideas and information without interference by a third party, except in the context of parliamentary debates and official proceedings. The scope of this freedom remains one of the most contentious issues in international human rights law, as different countries grapple with how to strike a balance between protection of individual freedoms and the prevention of real or perceived harm to others.

In a landmark case before the European Court of Human Rights, Mrs. A. vs. the United Kingdom, the judges held that the country had breached Article 6 of the Convention by allowing a search of her home under a warrant issued without a reasonable suspicion that an offence was being committed. The court found that the actions taken by the authorities had been inadequate in finding and seizing personal effects containing incriminating material. A key point to take away from this case is that just because a warrant has been issued for a search, law enforcement must still meet rigorous legal standards to substantiate the need for such intervention, ensuring accountability.

The European Convention on Human Rights established the right to freedom from arbitrary detention for anyone who has been kept in detention for more than 36 days in order to prevent him from committing a crime. Unless the detention was deemed justified as directed by the law, the detention shall be lawful only on the basis of which and to the extent necessary for the suspension of the detention.

The court of human rights is under increasing pressure to balance individual freedoms with the needs of national security measures following the a series of terrorist attacks in Europe and the ongoing Russia-Ukraine war has heightened concerns amongst diplomats about threats facing democracy and the rule of law in the justice system in some member states .

In 2017, the European Court of Human Rights delivered a landmark judgment in the case of Sejdini et al. v. the United Kingdom, ruling that a 48-hour waiting period for asylum seekers to lodge their claims was unlawful under the European Convention on Human Rights. The ruling cited Article 3 of the Convention, which prohibits 'unlawful and arbitrary seizure of persons in the enjoyment of their rights and freedoms' as grounds for denying Article 4 access to a court. The case revolved around the detention of an individual who was convicted of killing a man in a fit of rage and had been awaiting the imminent transfer to prison when he brought proceedings under Article 3 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The court ultimately ruled on December 9, 2020, that the United Kingdom discharged its duty of care regarding the state of the pending transfer and, having evaluated the detention against the required standards of humane treatment, the court determined that the measures taken could not limit that right and the detention breach was therefore unavoidable. The European Court of Human Rights (ECHR) has made numerous rulings regarding the right to a fair trial, including the presumption of innocence and the right to be assumed innocent until proven guilty. As the first step in this process, the accused must be informed of their rights as outlined in Article 6 of the ECHR. This includes the right to be informed of the charges against them, to be informed of the right to counsel, to be informed of the right to a fair and impartial judge, and to be informed of their right to proceed with or without counsel. Furthermore, the accused have the right to confront the prosecution and to have the right to examination during the trial. The European Court of Human Rights has ruled that this is an essential component of a fair trial, as it affords the accused an opportunity to defend their rights and to expose the evidence presented. In many cases, the ECHR has also ruled that the accused's confession, when obtained in violation of their rights, is not admissible as evidence in the proceedings.

“ The European Court of Human Rights has received numerous applications alleging the failure of the Estonian authorities to protect an individual's right to freedom of assembly as guarantee by the European Convention of Human Rights. The claimant sought damages for unlawful arrest, detention, and disciplinary action for his participation in a protest against government policies. The issue of balancing the right to freedom of assembly against public order and maintaining public safety remains a contentious issue in the European courtrooms , with many recent cases being brought on similar grounds . The ECHR has made judgements regarding this issue by taking into consideration the amount of time devoted by authorities to hear from the parties seeking damages regarding public safety concerns , whether the claimants had taken adequate measures to avoid disturbing the public order before attending the protests , and the role of media coverage in the events in question .

The European Court of Human Rights has concluded the application of Article 2 in the case of S. v. United Kingdom, regarding the detention at Poltava Military Airport. The applicant, a former commander of the Far Right party, alleged that his safety and protection from punishment by the authorities were not upheld, and made a complaint about being referred to immigration authorities while already on criminal charges. The ECHR ultimately rejected the case on the grounds that the laws and regulations in place provided reasonable grounds for the decision.

Although the margin of Appreciable risk surrounding the application of Article 10 in the case of Gülek v. Turkey stemmed from the applicant’s general criticism of the political atmosphere within the Turkish media, the Court held that such criticism was covered by the Article 10 protection, having considered the context of the political atmosphere in Turkey, the level of generality and the clear political scope of the publications.

The European Court of Human Rights has consistently upheld the right to a fair trial in its landmark ruling in the case of L.T. v Bulgaria and Cyprus in 2008. This judgment, which reaffirmed the principles established by the ECHR in X v United Kingdom in 1993, emphasizes the importance of ensuring that the accused has the opportunity to confront their accuser and defend themselves with the necessary means. The court ruled that the Bulgarian and Cypriot governments failed to provide the defendant, L.T., with adequate opportunities to prepare and defend himself during the hearing, thereby violating his right to a fair trial as guaranteed by Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The ECHR held that the combination of the language barrier and the lack of competent representation created an unequal contest of wills, which adversely affected the defendant's ability to present his arguments effectively. Ultimately, the court concluded that it was essential for the state to ensure that the defendant received a hearing that was fair, impartial, and conducted in accordance with due process. The judgment underscores the need for states to continually review and assess their trial procedures and ensure that they are in line with these principles.

The European Court of Human Rights has long been a beacon of justice for those whose human rights have been violated. The institution, established in 1949, has a robust legacy of protecting the rights of individuals across the continent. In case after case, the court has demonstrated its commitment to upholding the principles of human dignity and fundamental freedoms. recent rulings underscore the importance of protecting the environment and the right to health, as well as the need to combat discrimination and ensure equal treatment for all. Despite the challenges posed by complex legal issues, the ECHR remains a vital safeguard against humanRights abuses and a guarantor of justice in Europe. Through its tireless efforts, the European Court of Human Rights continues to promote the values of equality, justice, and human dignity for all.

On 21 November 2019, the Grand Chamber of the European Court of Human Rights, composed of 18 judges, delivered its judgment in the case of Müller v. Germany (Application No. 33622/19 ; 1 February 2020 ; CE [2020] ECDR 27). The judges held that Germany had failed to fulfil its obligations under Article 22 of the European Convention on Human Rights under the circumstances of mass surveillance under the 'Bundesagentur für Verfassungsfragen' (Federal Agency for the Matters of the Constitution) in 1949.

According to the European Court of Human Rights, a state is justified in detaining an individual as a preventive measure if there are reasonable grounds to believe that the person poses a threat to the safety of the public or to the stability of society. This ruling was reaffirmed in the landmark case of the Doble Hand case, where the court held that the detention of a British man by Norwegian authorities was reasonable in light of the circumstances following a violent crime. In fact, the ECHR found that the detention was proportionate and necessary in order to ensure the protection of the public and maintain public order. Conversely, the court emphasized that the detention should be conducted and justified by necessity, proportionality, and in the light of the specific situation, thereby ensuring accountability and upholding their rights as enshrined in the ECHR.
